metadata.source: Basic plant info expansion for cleaned master species

records.culinary_uses: Amber-orange berries eaten fresh when fully ripe or used in jam, syrup, desserts, and preserves.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional northern berry valued more as food than as a primary medicinal species.
records.notes: Low bog and peatland bramble with white flowers and a single raspberry-like aggregate fruit per stem.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. Unripe berries are hard and sour.

records.culinary_uses: Blue-black ripe fruits can be eaten fresh when soft or used in preserves, leather, and cooked fruit preparations.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional shrub with some medicinal history, though primarily a food fruit here.
records.notes: Shrub or small tree with opposite leaves, flat-topped white flower clusters, and dangling dark fruits that sweeten after frost or full ripening.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. Best when fully dark and soft.

records.culinary_uses: Soft red-to-dark fruits are edible and sometimes used fresh or cooked, though usually in small quantities.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food and ecological shrub rather than a major medicinal one in this app.
records.notes: Broad-leaved understory shrub with opposite fuzzy leaves and showy lacecap-like flower clusters.
records.edibility_detail: Edible fruit when ripe, though not as widely sought as other northern fruits.

records.culinary_uses: Bright clusters of fruit are usually cooked into jelly, syrup, or preserves rather than eaten casually raw.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional northern fruit tree with tonic and preserving uses, especially after frost or processing.
records.notes: Small tree with alternate compound leaves, white flower clusters, and orange-red berry clusters persisting into fall.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, but usually best processed. Raw fruits can be bitter or astringent.

records.culinary_uses: Small red berries are edible and mild, sometimes eaten fresh or used decoratively in small amounts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal woodland creeper with more historical use than strong culinary demand.
records.notes: Trailing evergreen forest-floor plant with opposite small leaves, white paired flowers, and red berries that often persist into winter.
records.edibility_detail: Edible but mild and not strongly flavored.

records.culinary_uses: Tart red berries can be made into sauces, preserves, whipped fruit dishes, and other processed foods.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional northern shrub with food and cultural uses.
records.notes: Shrub with opposite leaves that are often silvery or pale beneath, bearing red translucent fruits.
records.edibility_detail: Edible but usually better processed than eaten straight.

records.culinary_uses: Berry-like cones are used sparingly as seasoning, tea, or flavoring rather than bulk food.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional aromatic and urinary herb used in teas and preparations.
records.notes: Low shrub or small conifer with sharp needle-like leaves and blue berry-like cones.
records.edibility_detail: Used in small amounts as flavoring or tea. Not a bulk fruit.

records.culinary_uses: Aromatic leaves are mainly used for tea, seasoning, or infusion rather than as a vegetable.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional aromatic plant used in teas and simple preparations.
records.notes: Low shrub with narrow, deeply lobed fragrant leaves that resemble a fern but is not a fern.
records.edibility_detail: Used mainly as tea or flavoring.

records.culinary_uses: Leaves are used as tea in traditional northern practice rather than as a vegetable food.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tea plant with aromatic and medicinal history.
records.notes: Bog shrub with leathery alternate leaves and rusty underside, plus clusters of white flowers.
records.edibility_detail: Tea use is traditional, but this is a use-with-knowledge plant rather than a carefree daily beverage.
look_alikes: Bog rosemary and other bog shrubs

records.culinary_uses: Leaves, bulbs, and flowers can be used like mild onion or chives.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly culinary in this context.
records.notes: Native onion with grass-like leaves and nodding pink flower umbels.
records.edibility_detail: Edible allium with typical onion-garlic scent. Correct identification matters.
look_alikes: Death camas if not using smell carefully

records.culinary_uses: Leaves and bulbs can be used as pungent garlic-onion seasoning.
records.medicinal_uses: Mainly culinary.
records.notes: Slender onion-like allium with narrow hollow to chive-like leaves and strong garlic scent.
records.edibility_detail: Edible allium identified by smell. Do not trust appearance alone.

records.culinary_uses: Young shoots are used like tart rhubarb in desserts, sauces, chutneys, and preserves.
records.medicinal_uses: Also known from herbal medicine contexts, though this app treats it mainly as an edible shoot.
records.notes: Aggressive invasive with bamboo-like hollow stems, alternate leaves, and later sprays of pale flowers.
records.edibility_detail: Best when young and tender. Older stems become fibrous.

records.culinary_uses: Young leaves and shoots have traditional use as a cooked spring green.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food green here.
records.notes: Tall yellow-flowered composite with deeply cut leaves, often in moist rich ground.
records.edibility_detail: Best young. Older growth becomes tougher.

records.culinary_uses: Harvested grain used like rice in soups, pilafs, stuffings, and side dishes.
records.medicinal_uses: Food staple rather than medicinal plant here.
records.notes: Tall annual aquatic grass of shallow waters, producing loose grain-bearing panicles.
records.edibility_detail: Edible grain harvested at maturity and dried or processed.

records.culinary_uses: Starchy underwater tubers can be boiled, roasted, or dried after harvest from clean wetland sites.
records.medicinal_uses: Mainly a food plant.
records.notes: Aquatic plant with arrow-shaped leaves and white three-petaled flowers.
records.edibility_detail: Edible tubers. Harvest from clean water only.

records.culinary_uses: Seeds and underground parts have historical edible use after proper preparation.
records.medicinal_uses: Some traditional use exists, though it is mostly listed here for broad edible completeness.
records.notes: Floating-leaved aquatic plant with round leaves and showy white flowers.
records.edibility_detail: Edible with preparation, but not a casual beginner food.

records.culinary_uses: Seeds and some tender parts have reported edible use, especially in wetland-foraging traditions.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food-interest wetland plant here.
records.notes: Shallow-water plant with arrow to heart-shaped leaves and dense purple flower spikes.
records.edibility_detail: Edible with preparation and usually not a major beginner target.

records.culinary_uses: Root has traditional edible use, and young leaves or shoots may also be used cautiously in small amounts.
records.medicinal_uses: Well known from medicinal contexts, though here it is being tracked mainly for edible completeness.
records.notes: Biennial with basal rosette first year and tall flowering spike with yellow flowers the second.
records.edibility_detail: Edible with preparation; more of a broad-use species than a top-tier foraging food.

records.culinary_uses: Flowers and young leaves are used in teas, salads, and dried food blends.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional herb for tea and general tonic use.
records.notes: Common meadow legume with trifoliate leaves and rounded pink to purple flower heads.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, though often more useful as tea or adjunct food than a main vegetable.

records.culinary_uses: Fragrant flower clusters can be battered, fried, or used in sweets and teas.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a culinary flower here.
records.notes: Tree with alternate pinnate leaves, spines at nodes, and hanging white fragrant pea-like flowers.
records.edibility_detail: Flowers are edible, but other parts of the tree are not. Keep this distinction clear.

records.culinary_uses: Leaves and immature fruits are edible and can be used raw or cooked as a mucilaginous green.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional soothing herb with demulcent qualities.
records.notes: Low sprawling herb with rounded softly lobed leaves and small pale flowers.
records.edibility_detail: Edible and useful, especially as a mild cooked green.

records.culinary_uses: Young leaves and flower parts are edible, though usually in modest amounts.
records.medicinal_uses: Minor traditional use; mostly here for broad edible completeness.
records.notes: Common white-rayed daisy of roadsides, fields, and disturbed ground.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, but more a minor addition than a major calorie plant.

records.culinary_uses: Corms are edible, though tiny; young plants are also part of the spring-ephemeral edible tradition.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food-interest species rather than a major medicinal one.
records.notes: Delicate spring ephemeral with narrow leaves and pale pink-striped flowers on slender stems.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, but small size means low yield.
